sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: The digestive system is responsible for the mechanical and chemical breakdown of food into simpler molecules that can be absorbed by the body. It is found only in Eumetazoa — animals with true tissues.
content: Porifera (sponges) lack a digestive tract — digestion is entirely intracellular, carried out by choanocytes and amoebocytes.
From Cnidaria onwards, a distinct digestive cavity appears, and complexity increases progressively through the animal kingdom.
The evolution of the digestive system mirrors the transition from intracellular digestion (inside cells) to extracellular digestion (inside a cavity).

sections.heading: 1. Incomplete Digestive System
sections.text: An incomplete digestive system has only one opening that serves as both mouth and anus. Food enters and waste exits through the same pore — this is called a gastrovascular cavity.
content: Structure: A single blind-ended sac (gastrovascular cavity) with one opening.
Function: The same opening performs both ingestion (taking food in) and egestion (removing undigested waste).
Food movement is bidirectional — it can move in both directions inside the cavity.
Digestion is partly extracellular (inside the cavity) and partly intracellular (inside the cells lining the cavity).
sections.imageCaption: A gastrovascular cavity has one opening

sections.heading: 2. Complete Digestive System
sections.text: A complete digestive system has two separate openings — a mouth at the anterior end and an anus (or cloaca) at the posterior end. This forms a continuous tube called the alimentary canal.
content: Structure: A tube-like alimentary canal with distinct mouth and anus.
Function: Food moves in one direction only (unidirectional flow), greatly increasing digestive efficiency.
The canal becomes increasingly specialised — different regions (e.g. oesophagus, stomach, intestine) perform different functions.
Peristalsis (wave-like muscle contractions) moves food along the canal.
Accessory glands (salivary glands, liver, pancreas) appear in higher animals, adding digestive juices.
sections.imageCaption: An alimentary canal has two openings — mouth and anus
examples: Annelida — Earthworm (with specialised regions: pharynx, crop, gizzard)

sections.heading: Key Concept: Increasing Specialisation
sections.text: As animals evolved, the alimentary canal developed specialised regions. In earthworms, you can already see a distinct pharynx, oesophagus, crop (storage), gizzard (grinding), and intestine. In vertebrates, the stomach and intestine become highly specialised with enzymes, villi, and accessory glands.

rows: Anus or cloaca present at posterior end

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Respiration is the process by which organisms exchange gases — taking in oxygen (O₂) for cellular metabolism and releasing carbon dioxide (CO₂) as a waste product. The type of respiratory organ depends on the animal's habitat, body size, and level of organisation.
content: All respiratory surfaces share two features: they are thin (for easy diffusion) and moist (gases dissolve in water before crossing membranes).
In simple animals, the body surface itself is sufficient. In larger, more active animals, specialised respiratory organs evolved to increase the surface area available for gas exchange.
The driving force is always diffusion — gases move from regions of higher partial pressure to lower partial pressure (Fick's Law).

sections.heading: Body Surface Respiration
sections.text: The simplest form of respiration. Gas exchange occurs directly through the general body surface by simple diffusion. No specialised respiratory organs are needed.
content: The body wall must be thin and permeable to gases.
Oxygen dissolves in the moisture on the body surface and diffuses inward; CO₂ diffuses outward.
Only works in small or flattened animals with a high surface-area-to-volume ratio.
Examples: Porifera (sponges), Cnidaria, Platyhelminthes.

sections.heading: Cutaneous Respiration
sections.text: Respiration through the skin. The skin must be thin, moist, and richly supplied with blood capillaries to allow efficient gas exchange.
content: Skin glands secrete mucus to keep the surface moist at all times.
Blood flowing through dermal capillaries picks up O₂ and releases CO₂.
Often supplemented by other respiratory modes (lungs, buccopharyngeal cavity).
Examples: Annelida — Earthworm, Amphibia — Frog (dominant during hibernation).

sections.heading: Branchial Respiration
sections.text: Respiration using gills — highly vascularised, feathery outgrowths that extract dissolved oxygen from water. Gills provide an enormous surface area packed into a small space.
content: Gills may be external (exposed, e.g. in larval amphibians) or internal (protected by an operculum in bony fishes).
Water flows over the gill filaments, and oxygen diffuses into the blood while CO₂ diffuses out.
Counter-current flow in fish gills maximises oxygen extraction — water flows in one direction and blood flows in the opposite direction.
Examples: Arthropoda — Crustacea, Mollusca — Ctenidia, Chordata — Pisces.

sections.heading: Tracheal Respiration
sections.text: A unique system found in insects. Air enters through small pores called spiracles on the body surface, and is carried directly to the tissues through a branching network of air tubes called tracheae.
content: Tracheae branch into finer tracheoles that reach every cell directly.
Blood (haemolymph) does NOT transport oxygen — gases are delivered directly by the tracheae.
This is the most efficient respiratory system for small terrestrial animals, allowing rapid gas exchange.
Ventilation is aided by rhythmic body movements that pump air in and out.
Examples: Arthropoda — Insecta (cockroach, grasshopper, beetle).

sections.heading: Pulmonary Respiration
sections.text: Respiration through lungs — internal, sac-like organs with a large, moist surface area that is richly vascularised. Lungs became the dominant respiratory organ in terrestrial vertebrates.
content: Air is drawn in through the nose/mouth and travels via the trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles to the alveoli (in mammals).
Gas exchange occurs across the thin alveolar walls — O₂ enters the blood, and CO₂ is expelled.
Lung complexity increases from amphibians (simple sac-like lungs) → reptiles (partially septate) → mammals (highly alveolar, maximising surface area).
Birds have a unique flow-through lung system with air sacs, allowing continuous gas exchange during both inhalation and exhalation.
Examples: Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves, Mammalia.

sections.heading: Buccopharyngeal Respiration
sections.text: Gas exchange through the moist, vascularised lining of the buccal (mouth) and pharyngeal cavity. This is a supplementary mode of respiration used by amphibians.
content: The floor of the mouth rhythmically moves up and down, ventilating the buccopharyngeal cavity.
Oxygen diffuses through the thin mucosal lining into underlying blood capillaries.
Used especially when the frog is at rest on land.
Examples: Amphibia — Frogs (used alongside cutaneous and pulmonary respiration).

sections.heading: Key Concept: Evolutionary Trend
sections.text: Respiratory organs evolved from simple body surface diffusion in aquatic invertebrates to highly efficient lungs in terrestrial vertebrates. The key trend is increasing surface area, vascularisation, and protection of the respiratory surface from desiccation.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: The circulatory system is the internal transport network of the body. It distributes nutrients, oxygen, hormones, and immune cells to all tissues while collecting metabolic wastes for removal.
content: In primitive animals (Porifera, Cnidaria), there is no true circulatory system — transport occurs by water movement or diffusion.
As body complexity increased, dedicated circulatory organs evolved: blood, blood vessels, and a muscular heart.
The circulatory system evolved in two major stages: open circulation (blood leaves vessels and bathes tissues directly) → closed circulation (blood always remains inside vessels).
In vertebrates, the heart evolved from a simple two-chambered pump in fishes to a fully four-chambered heart in birds and mammals.

sections.heading: Type I – Without Blood (Water as Medium)
sections.text: The most primitive form of internal transport. No blood or blood vessels are present — water serves as the transport medium.
content: Water circulates through internal channels, carrying dissolved oxygen, nutrients, and waste.
This system is adequate for small animals with limited metabolic demands.

sections.heading: Water Canal System
content: Present only in Porifera.
Helps in feeding, respiration, excretion, and reproduction.

sections.heading: Gastrovascular System
content: Present in Cnidaria and Ctenophora.
Same cavity for digestion and distribution.

sections.heading: Water Vascular System
content: Present in Echinoderms.
Helps in locomotion, respiration, and feeding.

sections.heading: Type II – With Blood
content: Blood is the medium of transportation

sections.heading: Circulatory Pathway – Heart
content: All vertebrates possess a muscular, chambered heart.
The number of chambers depends on the type of circulation.

sections.heading: The heart may have:
content: 1 or 2 atria (auricles)
1 or 2 ventricles

sections.heading: Single Circulation System
sections.text: In single circulation, blood passes through the heart only once during one complete cycle.
content: The heart pumps deoxygenated blood to the gills
Blood becomes oxygenated in the gills
Oxygenated blood flows to body tissues
Deoxygenated blood returns to heart.

sections.heading: fish heart
content: Functionally two-chambered (one atrium + one ventricle)
Also includes sinus venosus and bulbus/conus arteriosus
Exception: Lungfish → three-chambered heart

sections.heading: Incomplete Double Circulation (3-Chambered)
sections.text: In incomplete double circulation, blood passes through the heart twice, but oxygenated and deoxygenated blood mix partially.
content: Left atrium → oxygenated blood
Right atrium → deoxygenated blood
Both enter a single ventricle
Mixed blood is pumped to the body
examples: Reptiles have an incomplete interventricular septum

sections.heading: Double Circulation (4-Chambered)
sections.text: In double circulation, blood passes through the heart twice, and oxygenated and deoxygenated blood never mix.
content: Pulmonary circulation – heart → lungs → heart
Systemic circulation – heart → body → heart

sections.heading: Heart Structure
content: Four-chambered heart
Inter-auricular septum
Inter-ventricular septum

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Excretion is the process by which an organism removes metabolic waste products — especially nitrogenous wastes produced by protein and nucleic acid metabolism. It also helps maintain the internal chemical balance of the body (homeostasis).
content: The primary nitrogenous waste products are ammonia (NH₃), urea, and uric acid. Animals are classified based on their dominant nitrogenous waste:
Ammonotelic animals excrete ammonia (highly toxic, requires lots of water) — e.g. aquatic invertebrates, bony fishes.
Ureotelic animals excrete urea (less toxic, needs less water) — e.g. amphibians, mammals.
Uricotelic animals excrete uric acid (least toxic, conserves water) — e.g. insects, reptiles, birds.
Excretory organs progressively evolved from simple diffusion → flame cells → nephridia → Malpighian tubules → kidneys.

sections.heading: Stage 1: Excretion by Diffusion (No Excretory Organs)
sections.text: Phyla: Porifera, Cnidaria, Ctenophora, Echinodermata
content: These animals do not possess any excretory organs
Metabolic wastes (mainly ammonia) are removed by simple diffusion.
Waste diffuses out through:General body surface,Tube feet and papulae (in echinoderms)
Aquatic habitat provides abundant water for dilution
Mode of excretion: Diffusion
Nature: Most primitive condition

sections.heading: Stage 2: Protonephridia (Primitive Tubular Excretory System)
sections.text: Phyla: Platyhelminthes, Some Annelids, Cephalochordata
content: Here, the first tubular excretory structures appear.
Function: Excretion + osmoregulation
Significance: First organized excretory system

sections.heading: Flame cells (with cilia)
content: Found in Platyhelminthes and some annelids.
Cilia beat like a flame and drive waste fluid out.

sections.heading: Solenocytes (with flagella)
content: Found in Cephalochordata
Function similar to flame cells but use flagella

sections.heading: Stage 3: Glandular Excretory Structures
content: Nature: Simple but specialized
Intermediate evolutionary stage

sections.heading: Phylum: Nematoda (Aschelminthes)
content: Excretion carried out by renette cells
These are large glandular cells
Also called intracellular tubes
Open to the exterior through an excretory pore

sections.heading: Stage 4: Metanephridia (True Nephridia)
sections.text: Phyla: Annelida, Non-cephalopod Mollusca
content: Excretory organs are metanephridia (nephridia)
Segmentally arranged tubular structures
Highly efficient removal of wastes
Better regulation of body fluids

sections.heading: Each nephridium has:
content: Nephrostome (funnel)
Tubule
Nephridiopore

sections.heading: Stage 5: Specialized Excretory Organs in Arthropods
sections.text: Phyla: Arthropods
content: Different classes show different excretory organs, indicating specialization.
Adapted to terrestrial life
Efficient water conservation

sections.heading: Stage 6: Kidney – Advanced Excretory Organ
sections.text: Phyla: Cephalopod Mollusca & Vertebrata
content: Excretion carried out by well-developed kidneys
Nephron is the structural and functional unit
Kidney structure varies according to habitat

sections.heading: Marine fishes
content: Aglomerular kidney
Produce very little isoosmotic urine

sections.heading: Freshwater fishes & Amphibians
content: No Henle’s loop
Produce large amount of dilute (hypoosmotic) urine

sections.heading: Reptiles
content: Reduced or absent glomeruli
No Henle’s loop
Produce very little hypotonic urine

sections.heading: Mammals
content: Long loop of Henle
Produce concentrated (hyperosmotic) urine

sections.heading: Types of Excretion (Nitrogenous Waste)

content.text: Living organisms eliminate nitrogenous wastes formed during protein and nucleic acid metabolism. The major nitrogenous waste products are:

content: Other nitrogenous wastes include: Trimethylamine oxide (TMO), guanine, hippuric acid, creatinine, creatine, purines, pyrimidines, and pteridines.

sections.heading: 1.Ammonotelism
content: Animals that excrete ammonia are called ammonotelic.

sections.heading: Terrestrial Adaptation
sections.text: With the transition to land, animals evolved less toxic nitrogenous wastes (urea and uric acid) to conserve water.

sections.heading: 2. Ureotelism
content: Animals that excrete urea are called ureotelic.

sections.heading: 3.Uricotelism
content: Animals that excrete uric acid are called uricotelic

sections.heading: Significance in Shelled Eggs
content: If embryos inside shelled eggs produced ammonia or urea, these wastes would accumulate to toxic levels.

content.text: This problem is solved because such animals are uricotelic—uric acid precipitates due to its insolubility and remains harmlessly within the egg shell.

sections.heading: 4. Guanotelism
content: Animals that excrete guanine are called guanotelic.

rows: Aquatic invertebrates, freshwater bony fishes, larval amphibians, crustaceans

sections.heading: One-line Memory Summary
content: Ammonia → very toxic → lots of water → aquatic
Urea → moderately toxic → some water saving → land & semi-aquatic
Uric acid / Guanine → least toxic → maximum water saving → dry land

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: The skeleton is the rigid or semi-rigid framework that gives the body shape, support, and protection. It also plays a crucial role in locomotion by providing surfaces for muscle attachment.
content: Skeletons are broadly classified into two types: exoskeleton (outside the body) and endoskeleton (inside the body).
Simpler animals (Porifera, Cnidaria) have spicules or a hydrostatic skeleton (fluid-filled cavity providing support).
Higher invertebrates like arthropods developed a chitinous exoskeleton, while vertebrates possess a bony or cartilaginous endoskeleton.
A key evolutionary trend is the shift from external to internal skeletons — this allows greater body size, more efficient movement, and continuous growth.

sections.heading: Exoskeleton
sections.text: An exoskeleton is a skeleton that is present outside the body. It is usually formed of dead, non-living structures secreted by the epidermis.
content: Note: In mammals, these structures are epidermal derivatives, not true bones.
examples: Hair, nails, claws, horns, and hooves in mammals

sections.heading: Endoskeleton
sections.text: An endoskeleton is a skeleton that is present inside the body. It is made of living tissues, mainly cartilage and bone.
examples: Cartilaginous and bony skeleton of vertebrates (fish, frog, humans)

rows: Does not grow — must be shed (moulting)

sections.heading: Stomochord
sections.text: The stomochord is a hollow, flexible tube found only in hemichordates. It lies in the anterior region of the body and provides support to the proboscis.

sections.heading: Notochord
sections.text: The notochord is a rod-like, flexible structure derived from the mesoderm. It forms during embryonic development and lies on the dorsal side of the body.
content: Animals that possess a notochord at any stage of life are called chordates.
Animals that never form a notochord are called non-chordates.

sections.heading: Vertebrates
sections.text: In many chordates, the notochord is present only during embryonic stages. Later, it is replaced by a vertebral column (backbone) made of bone or cartilage.
content: These animals are called vertebrates.

sections.heading: Key Concept: Skeleton Evolution
sections.text: The skeleton evolved from simple spicules and hydrostatic support in invertebrates to an internal bony framework in vertebrates. The notochord — the first true axial skeleton — was a breakthrough that enabled the radiation of chordates.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Metamerism (segmentation) is the division of the animal body into a series of similar, repeating units along the head-to-tail axis. Each unit is called a metamere or somite.
content: Segmentation is one of the most important body plans in animal evolution — it allows greater flexibility, efficient locomotion, and regional specialisation.
Each metamere can contain its own set of muscles, nerves, blood vessels, and excretory organs — this repetition is called serial homology.

content.text: Metamerism is found in three highly organized phyla:

content: In these animals, segmentation may be present externally, internally, or both.
When segmentation is present both externally and internally, it is called true (metameric) segmentation.

sections.heading: Types of Metamerism
sections.text: Metamerism is classified into two main types based on how similar the segments are:

sections.heading: Homonomous Metamerism
sections.text: All segments are essentially identical in structure and function — like a chain of identical links.
content: Each metamere contains the same set of organs (nephridia, ganglia, setae).
Simple and repetitive — damage to one segment doesn't critically affect the whole animal.
examples: Annelida — Earthworm (each segment has nephridia, setae, ganglia)

sections.heading: Heteronomous Metamerism
sections.text: Segments are dissimilar and have become specialised for different functions — this is called tagmosis (fusion and specialisation of segments into body regions).
content: Body segments are grouped into distinct functional regions (tagmata) — e.g. head, thorax, abdomen.
Different tagmata perform different roles: the head for sensing, the thorax for locomotion, and the abdomen for reproduction.
This is considered more advanced than homonomous metamerism.
examples: Arthropoda — Insects (head + thorax + abdomen), Crabs (cephalothorax + abdomen)
Chordata — Vertebrates (internal segmentation: vertebrae, spinal nerves, myotomes)

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: The nervous system controls and coordinates all body activities through electrical impulses and chemical signals. It evolved from simple cellular coordination in sponges to a highly centralised brain-and-spinal-cord system in vertebrates.
content: The nervous system is ectodermal in origin and is composed of specialised cells called neurons, which detect, receive, and transmit stimuli.
Neurons communicate at junctions called synapses using chemical messengers called neurotransmitters (e.g. acetylcholine, dopamine).
A key evolutionary trend is cephalization — the concentration of nervous tissue at the anterior (head) end, forming a brain.
Complexity increases: no nervous system (Porifera) → diffuse nerve net (Cnidaria) → ventral nerve cord with ganglia (Annelida, Arthropoda) → dorsal hollow nerve cord with brain (Chordata).

sections.heading: Porifera (Sponges)
content: Nervous system: Absent
No neurons or nerve tissues are present.
Body activities are controlled by simple cell-to-cell coordination.
sections.text: This represents the most primitive condition.

sections.heading: Cnidaria (Coelenterata) & Ctenophora
content: Nervous system consists of a loose network of nerve fibers, called a nerve net.
Nerve cells are present on both sides of the mesoglea.
No brain or ganglia.
Impulses can travel in all directions.

sections.heading: Beginning of Centralization (Nerve Ring + Nerve Cords)
Platyhelminthes & Aschelminthes
content: Nervous system shows early centralization.
A nerve ring is present around the anterior part of the gut.
From the nerve ring, many longitudinal nerve cords extend backward.
Often described as a ladder-like nervous system.
sections.text: This allows directional movement and better coordination.

sections.heading: Annelida & Arthropoda
content: Nervous system is well developed and centralized.
A nerve ring surrounds the anterior part of the gut.
A double, solid, mid-ventral nerve cord is present.
The nerve cord has segmentally arranged ganglia.
sections.text: This arrangement provides precise control of segmented body movements.

sections.heading: Mollusca
isSubtopic: Nervous system consists of paired ganglia.
sections.text: This allows higher coordination and learning ability.

sections.heading: Echinodermata
content: Nervous system shows radial symmetry.
No true brain.
sections.text: Suitable for radially symmetrical body plan.

sections.heading: Diffuse with Nerve Cords (Primitive Chordate Condition)
Hemichordata
content: Still lacks true chordate organization.
sections.text: Represents a transitional condition.

sections.heading: Vertebrates (Chordata)
content: Nervous system is most complex and centralized.
Brain is enclosed in the cranium.
Spinal cord is protected by the vertebral column.
sections.text: This allows advanced behavior, learning, and rapid responses.

sections.heading: Reproductive System in Animals
sections.text: Reproduction is the most fundamental biological drive — it ensures the continuation of species and maintains population size across generations. Animals reproduce through two main strategies: asexual and sexual reproduction.
content: Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring (clones) from a single parent — fast but no genetic variation.
Sexual reproduction involves fusion of gametes from two parents — slower but generates genetic diversity, which is essential for evolution and adaptation.
Most higher animals reproduce exclusively by sexual reproduction, while many lower animals can use both modes.

sections.heading: Asexual Reproduction
sections.text: In asexual reproduction, a single parent produces new individuals without gamete formation or fusion. The offspring are genetically identical to the parent (clones). This type is common in simpler animals and is favoured in stable environments.
content: No meiosis or fertilisation is involved — reproduction occurs by mitosis.
Advantages: rapid multiplication, requires only one parent, guaranteed reproduction.
Disadvantages: no genetic variation — entire population is vulnerable to the same disease or environmental change.

sections.heading: Binary Fission
sections.text: One organism divides into two equal individuals.

sections.heading: Multiple Fission
sections.text: One parent produces many offspring at once.

sections.heading: Budding
sections.text: A small outgrowth (bud) forms on the parent and later separates.

sections.heading: Fragmentation
sections.text: The body breaks into pieces, and each piece develops into a new individual.

sections.heading: Sexual Reproduction
content: In sexual reproduction, two parents are usually involved.
This type of reproduction is common in higher animals and leads to genetic variation.

sections.heading: Gametes and Gonads
content: Gametes are sex cells.
Male gamete: Sperm
Female gamete: Ovum (egg)

sections.heading: Gametes are produced in special organs called gonads:
content: Testes → produce sperms
Ovaries → produce ova

sections.heading: Unisexual (Dioecious) Animals
content: Male and female gonads are present in different individuals.
Each organism is either male or female.

sections.heading: Bisexual (Monoecious / Hermaphrodite) Animals
content: Both testes and ovaries are present in the same individual.
Self-fertilization or cross-fertilization may occur.

sections.heading: Evolutionary Trend in Reproduction
content: Lower animals → mainly asexual reproduction
Higher animals → sexual reproduction
Sexual reproduction increases variation, adaptability, and survival

rows: Asexual by budding, fragmentation; sexual by gametes
Budding common (Hydra); alternation of generations in some
Metamorphosis common; insects show high specialization

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Fertilization (syngamy) is the fusion of a male gamete (sperm) with a female gamete (ovum) to form a diploid zygote — the first cell of a new individual. It restores the diploid chromosome number.
content: Fertilization involves two key events: activation of the egg (triggering development) and combination of paternal and maternal genetic material.
Based on where fertilization occurs, it is classified as external (outside the body, in water) or internal (inside the female reproductive tract).
The evolutionary trend is from external → internal fertilization, reflecting increasing protection of gametes and developing embryos.

sections.heading: 3.Self-Fertilization
sections.text: Self-fertilization occurs when the sperm and egg produced by the same individual fuse together.
content: Seen in bisexual (hermaphrodite) animals
Ensures reproduction even when mates are unavailable

sections.heading: 4.Cross-Fertilization
sections.text: Cross-fertilization occurs when the gametes from two different individuals fuse.
content: Most common type in animals
Leads to genetic variation
Fertilization evolved from external (aquatic) → internal (terrestrial) with increasing protection of gametes and embryos

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Development refers to the series of changes by which a zygote grows and differentiates into an adult animal. The process involves cell division (cleavage), cell differentiation, morphogenesis (formation of body shape), and growth.
content: The type of development depends on the amount and distribution of yolk in the egg, the habitat, and evolutionary history.
Based on modes of birth, animals are classified as oviparous (egg-laying), viviparous (live birth), or ovoviviparous (eggs retained inside the body).
Based on life cycle, development can be direct (young resemble adults) or indirect (involving a larval stage and metamorphosis).

sections.heading: 1.Oviparous Animals
sections.text: Oviparous animals lay eggs, and development of the embryo takes place outside the body of the mother.
content: Eggs may be laid after external or internal fertilization
Embryo is nourished by yolk

sections.heading: 2.Viviparous Animals
sections.text: Viviparous animals give birth to young ones.
contents: The embryo develops inside the mother
Nourishment is obtained directly from the mother (placenta in mammals)

sections.heading: 3.Ovoviviparous Animals
sections.text: In ovoviviparous animals:
content: Eggs are retained inside the mother’s body
Embryo develops inside the egg
There is no direct nourishment from the mother
Young ones hatch inside the mother and are then released

sections.heading: Types of Development (Based on Life Cycle)
sections.text: Development is further classified into two types based on whether a larval stage is present.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Animals differ fundamentally in their ability to regulate body temperature. This ability determines their activity levels, habitat range, and survival strategies. Based on thermoregulation, animals are classified into two broad groups.
content: Poikilothermic (ectothermic) — body temperature varies with the environment ('cold-blooded').
Homeothermic (endothermic) — body temperature is maintained constant by internal metabolic heat ('warm-blooded').
Thermoregulation is closely linked to metabolic rate — endotherms have a much higher basal metabolic rate than ectotherms.
Some animals exhibit behavioural adaptations to cope with temperature extremes — hibernation (winter dormancy) and aestivation (summer dormancy).

sections.heading: Poikilothermic (Ectothermic) Animals
sections.text: Poikilothermic animals cannot generate enough metabolic heat to maintain a constant body temperature. Their body temperature fluctuates with the surrounding environment.
content: Also called ectothermic or 'cold-blooded' animals.
Body temperature depends on external heat sources
Mostly found in aquatic or warm environments
examples: A lizard becomes active in sunlight and sluggish in cold.

sections.heading: Homeothermic (Endothermic) Animals
sections.text: Homeothermic animals maintain a constant body temperature, regardless of changes in environmental temperature.
content: Also called endothermic animals
Body heat is produced internally by metabolism
Requires more energy
examples: Humans maintain ~37 °C body temperature in all seasons

sections.heading: Salinity Tolerance in Animals
sections.text: Animals also differ in their ability to tolerate changes in salt concentration of the environment.

sections.heading: Stenohaline Animals
sections.text: Stenohaline animals can tolerate only a narrow range of salinity.
content: Mostly marine or freshwater animals
Cannot survive drastic salinity changes

sections.heading: Euryhaline Animals
sections.text: Euryhaline animals can tolerate a wide range of salinity changes.
content: Can live in both freshwater and seawater
Common in estuaries
